steps.title: Use the conclusion as the target
steps.content: Marsh and Nair conclude that delayed autumn freezing gives invasive species an advantage in Alaska. A supporting finding must therefore connect the delay to a greater benefit for the invasive P. aviculare than for the native C. canadensis. It is not enough to show that both plants respond to warmer autumn conditions; the result has to show a stronger response by the invasive species.

steps.title: Compare each finding
steps.content: Choice A says P. aviculare stopped producing leaves sooner than C. canadensis, which weakens the idea of an invasive advantage. Choice B says neither species varied with the timing of subfreezing temperatures, so it shows no advantage from the delay. Choice D says both species produced more leaves, but it also introduces early spring onset as another possible cause. Choice C says both species produced leaves later into autumn, and the extension was much greater for P. aviculare, directly supporting the conclusion. The answer is C.
